AllFurthestNeighborsTraits_2.name: AllFurthestNeighborsTraits_2
AllFurthestNeighborsTraits_2.brief: The concept AllFurthestNeighborsTraits_2 defines types and operations needed to compute all furthest neighbors for the vertices of a convex polygon using the function all_furthest_neighbors_2() .
AllFurthestNeighborsTraits_2.detailed: Has models CGAL::Cartesian<FieldNumberType> CGAL::Homogeneous<RingNumberType> CGAL::Simple_cartesian<FieldNumberType> CGAL::Simple_homogeneous<RingNumberType> CGAL::all_furthest_neighbors_2() AllFurthestNeighborsTraits_2::Less_xy_2 and AllFurthestNeighborsTraits_2::Orientation_2 are used for (expensive) precondition checking only. Therefore, they need not to be specified, in case that precondition checking is disabled.

CGAL::Polytope_distance_d.name: CGAL::Polytope_distance_d
CGAL::Polytope_distance_d.brief: An object of the class Polytope_distance_d represents the (squared) distance between two convex polytopes, given as the convex hulls of two finite point sets in $ d$ -dimensional Euclidean space $ \E^d$ .
CGAL::Polytope_distance_d.detailed: For point sets $ P$ and $ Q$ we denote by $ pd(P,Q)$ the distance between the convex hulls of $ P$ and $ Q$ . Note that $ pd(P,Q)$ can be degenerate, i.e. $ pd(P,Q)=\infty$ if $ P$ or $ Q$ is empty. Two inclusion-minimal subsets $ S_P$ of $ P$ and $ S_Q$ of $ Q$ with $ pd(S_P,S_Q)=pd(P,Q)$ are called pair of support sets , the points in $ S_P$ and $ S_Q$ are the support points . A pair of support sets has size at most $ d+2$ (by size we mean $ |S_P|+|S_Q|$ ). The distance between the two polytopes is realized by a pair of points $ p$ and $ q$ lying in the convex hull of $ S_P$ and $ S_Q$ , respectively, i.e. $ \sqrt{||p-q||}=pd(P,Q)$ . In general, neither the support sets nor the realizing points are necessarily unique. The underlying algorithm can cope with all kinds of input, e.g. $ P$ and $ Q$ may be in non-convex position or points may occur more than once. The algorithm computes a pair of support sets $ S_P$ and $ S_Q$ with realizing points $ p$ and $ q$ which remain fixed until the next set, insert, or clear operation. Traits must be a model for PolytopeDistanceDTraits . We provide the models Polytope_distance_d_traits_2 , Polytope_distance_d_traits_3 , and Polytope_distance_d_traits_d using the two-, three-, and $ d$ -dimensional CGAL kernel, respectively. CGAL::Polytope_distance_d_traits_2 <K, ET ,NT> CGAL::Polytope_distance_d_traits_3 <K, ET ,NT> CGAL::Polytope_distance_d_traits_d <K, ET ,NT> PolytopeDistanceDTraits Implementation The problem of finding the distance between two convex polytopes given as the convex hulls of two finite point sets can be formulated as an optimization problem with linear constraints and a convex quadratic objective function. The solution is obtained using our exact solver for quadratic programs [2] . The creation time is almost always linear in the number of points. Access functions and predicates take constant time, inserting a point might take up to linear time. The clear operation and the check for validity each take linear time.

members.brief: typedef to Traits::Point_d .
members.detailed: Point type used to represent the input points.

members.name: Point_iterator

members.name: Support_point_iterator

members.name: Support_point_index_iterator
members.brief: non-mutable model of the STL concept RandomAccessIterator with value type int .

members.name: Coordinate_iterator
members.brief: non-mutable model of the STL concept RandomAccessIterator with value type ET .
members.detailed: Used to access the coordinates of the realizing points.

members.name: Polytope_distance_d
members.brief: initializes poly_dist to $ pd(\emptyset, \emptyset)$ .

members.name: Polytope_distance_d
members.brief: initializes poly_dist to $ pd(P,Q)$ with $ P$ and $ Q$ being the sets of points in the range [ p_first , p_last ) and [ q_first , q_last ), respectively.
members.detailed: InputIterator1 has Point as value type. InputIterator2 has Point as value type. All points have the same dimension. If verbose is set to $ 1$ , $ 2$ , or $ 3$ then some, more, or full verbose output of the underlying solver for quadratic programs is written to stream , resp.
members.argsstring: (InputIterator1 p_first, InputIterator1 p_last, InputIterator2 q_first, InputIterator2 q_last, const Traits &traits=Traits(), int verbose=0, std::ostream &stream=std::cout)

members.name: ambient_dimension
members.brief: returns the dimension of the points in $ P$ and $ Q$ .
members.detailed: If poly_dist is $ pd(\emptyset, \emptyset)$ , the ambient dimension is $ -1$ .

members.name: number_of_points
members.brief: returns the number of all points of poly_dist , i.e. $ |P|+|Q|$ .

members.name: number_of_points_p
members.brief: returns the number of points in $ P$ .

members.name: number_of_points_q
members.brief: returns the number of points in $ Q$ .

members.name: number_of_support_points
members.brief: returns the number of support points of poly_dist , i.e. $ |S_P|+|S_Q|$ .

members.name: number_of_support_points_p
members.brief: returns the number of support points in $ S_P$ .

members.name: number_of_support_points_q
members.brief: returns the number of support points in $ S_Q$ .

members.name: points_p_begin
members.brief: returns an iterator referring to the first point in $ P$ .

members.name: points_p_end
members.brief: returns the corresponding past-the-end iterator.

members.name: points_q_begin
members.brief: returns an iterator referring to the first point in $ Q$ .

members.name: points_q_end
members.brief: returns the corresponding past-the-end iterator.

members.name: support_points_p_begin

members.name: support_points_p_end
members.brief: returns the corresponding past-the-end iterator.

members.name: support_points_q_begin
members.brief: returns an iterator referring to the first support point in $ S_Q$ .

members.name: support_points_q_end
members.brief: returns the corresponding past-the-end iterator.

members.name: support_points_p_indices_begin
members.brief: returns an iterator referring to the index of the first support point in $ P$ .

members.name: support_points_p_indices_end
members.brief: returns the corresponding past-the-end iterator.

members.name: support_points_q_indices_begin
members.brief: returns an iterator referring to the index of the first support point in $ Q$ .

members.name: support_points_q_indices_end
members.brief: returns the corresponding past-the-end iterator.

members.name: realizing_point_p
members.brief: returns the realizing point of $ P$ .
members.detailed: An implicit conversion from ET to RT must be available. $ pd(P,Q)$ is finite.

members.name: realizing_point_q
members.brief: returns the realizing point of $ Q$ .
members.detailed: An implicit conversion from ET to RT must be available. $ pd(P,Q)$ is finite.

members.name: squared_distance
members.brief: returns the squared distance of poly_dist , i.e. $ (pd(P,Q))^2$ .
members.detailed: An implicit conversion from ET to RT must be available. $ pd(P,Q)$ is finite.

members.name: realizing_point_p_coordinates_begin
members.brief: returns an iterator referring to the first coordinate of the realizing point of $ P$ .
members.detailed: The coordinates have a rational representation, i.e. the first $ d$ elements of the iterator range are the numerators and the $ (d\!+\!1)$ -st element is the common denominator.

members.name: realizing_point_p_coordinates_end
members.brief: returns the corresponding past-the-end iterator.

members.name: realizing_point_q_coordinates_begin
members.brief: returns an iterator referring to the first coordinate of the realizing point of $ Q$ .
members.detailed: The coordinates have a rational representation, i.e. the first $ d$ elements of the iterator range are the numerators and the $ (d\!+\!1)$ -st element is the common denominator.

members.name: realizing_point_q_coordinates_end
members.brief: returns the corresponding past-the-end iterator.

members.name: squared_distance_numerator
members.brief: returns the numerator of the squared distance of poly_dist .

members.name: squared_distance_denominator
members.brief: returns the denominator of the squared distance of poly_dist .

members.name: is_finite
members.brief: returns true , if $ pd(P,Q)$ is finite, i.e. none of the two polytopes is empty.

members.name: is_zero
members.brief: returns true , if $ pd(P,Q)$ is zero, i.e. the two polytopes intersect (this implies degeneracy).

members.name: is_degenerate
members.brief: returns true , iff $ pd(P,Q)$ is degenerate, i.e. $ pd(P,Q)$ is not finite.

members.name: clear
members.brief: resets poly_dist to $ pd(\emptyset, \emptyset)$ .

members.name: set
members.brief: sets poly_dist to $ pd(P,Q)$ with $ P$ and $ Q$ being the sets of points in the ranges [ p_first , p_last ) and [ q_first , q_last ), respectively.
members.detailed: InputIterator1 has Point as value type. InputIterator2 has Point as value type. All points have the same dimension.
members.argsstring: (InputIterator1 p_first, InputIterator1 p_last, InputIterator2 q_first, InputIterator2 q_last)

members.name: set_p
members.brief: sets poly_dist to $ pd(P,Q)$ with $ P$ being the set of points in the range [ p_first , p_last ) ( $ Q$ remains unchanged).
members.detailed: InputIterator has Point as value type. All points in $ P$ have dimension poly_dist``. ambient_dimension() if $ Q$ is not empty.

members.name: set_q
members.brief: sets poly_dist to $ pd(P,Q)$ with $ Q$ being the set of points in the range [ q_first , q_last ) ( $ P$ remains unchanged).
members.detailed: InputIterator has Point as value type. All points in $ Q$ have dimension poly_dist``. ambient_dimension() if $ P$ is not empty.

members.name: insert_p
members.brief: inserts p into $ P$ .
members.detailed: The dimension of p equals poly_dist``. ambient_dimension() if poly_dist is not $ pd(\emptyset, \emptyset)$ .

members.name: insert_q
members.brief: inserts q into $ Q$ .
members.detailed: The dimension of q equals poly_dist``. ambient_dimension() if poly_dist is not $ pd(\emptyset, \emptyset)$ .

members.name: insert
members.brief: inserts the points in the range [ p_first , p_last ) and [ q_first , q_last ) into $ P$ and $ Q$ , respectively, and recomputes the (squared) distance.
members.detailed: InputIterator1 has Point as value type. InputIterator2 has Point as value type. All points have the same dimension. If poly_dist is not $ pd(\emptyset, \emptyset)$ , this dimension must be equal to poly_dist``. ambient_dimension() .
members.argsstring: (InputIterator1 p_first, InputIterator1 p_last, InputIterator2 q_first, InputIterator2 q_last)

members.name: insert_p
members.brief: inserts the points in the range [ p_first , p_last ) into $ P$ and recomputes the (squared) distance ( $ Q$ remains unchanged).
members.detailed: InputIterator has Point as value type. All points have the same dimension. If poly_dist is not empty, this dimension must be equal to poly_dist``. ambient_dimension() .

members.name: insert_q
members.brief: inserts the points in the range [ q_first , q_last ) into $ Q$ and recomputes the (squared) distance ( $ P$ remains unchanged).
members.detailed: InputIterator has Point as value type. All points have the same dimension. If poly_dist is not empty, this dimension must be equal to poly_dist``. ambient_dimension() .

members.name: is_valid
members.brief: returns true , iff poly_dist is valid.
members.detailed: If verbose is true , some messages concerning the performed checks are written to standard error stream. The second parameter level is not used, we provide it only for consistency with interfaces of other classes. An object poly_dist is valid, iff $ ldots$ poly_dist contains all points of its defining set $ P$ , poly_dist is the smallest sphere containing its support set $ S$ , and $ S$ is minimal, i.e. no support point is redundant.

members.name: traits
members.brief: returns a const reference to the traits class object.

members.name: operator<<
members.brief: writes poly_dist to output stream os .
members.detailed: An overload of operator<< must be defined for Point_d .

members.name: operator>>
members.brief: reads poly_dist from input stream is .
members.detailed: An overload of operator>> must be defined for Point_d .

CGAL::Width_3.name: CGAL::Width_3
CGAL::Width_3.brief: Given a set of points $ \mathcal{S}=\left\{p_1,\ldots , p_n\right\}$ in $ \mathbb{R}^3$ .
CGAL::Width_3.detailed: The width of $ \mathcal{S}$ , denoted as $ \mathcal{W(S)}$ , is defined as the minimum distance between two parallel planes of support of $ \mathit{conv(\mathcal{S})}$ ; where $ \mathit{conv(\mathcal{S})}$ denotes the convex hull of $ \mathcal{S}$ . The width in direction $ \mathbf{d}$ , denoted as $ \mathcal{W}_d\mathcal{(S)}$ , is the distance between two parallel planes of support of $ \mathit{conv(\mathcal{S})}$ , which are orthogonal to $ \mathbf{d}$ . Subject to the applications of the width algorithm, several objects might be interesting: The two parallel planes of support such that the distance between them is as small as possible. These planes are called width-planes in further considerations. The width $ \mathcal{W(S)}$ , i.e., the distance between the width-planes. The direction $ \mathbf{d}_{opt}$ such that $ \mathcal{W(S)}=\mathcal{W}_{d_{opt}}\mathcal{(S)}$ There might be several optimal build directions. Hence neither the width-planes nor the direction $ \mathbf{d}_{opt}$ are unique - only the width is. There may also be duplicates reported, different vectors having the same or the opposite directions. Traits must be a model for WidthTraits_3 . We provide the model Width_default_traits_3 <Kernel> based on a three-dimensional CGAL kernel. CGAL::Width_default_traits_3 <K> WidthTraits_3 Implementation Since the width of the point set $ \mathcal{S}$ and the width of the convex hull of $ \mathcal{S}$ ( $ \mathit{conv(\mathcal{S})}$ ) is the same, the algorithm uses the 3D convex hull algorithm CGAL provides. The width-algorithm is not incremental and therefore inserting and erasing points cause not an 'automatic' update of the width. Instead you have to run the width-algorithm again even if the point set is extended by only one new point. Large Numbers. Because there is no need for dividing values during the algorithm, the numbers can get really huge (all the computations are made using a lot of multiplications). Therefore it is strongly recommended to use a number type that can handle numbers of arbitrary length (e.g., Exact_integer in combination with the homogeneous representation of the points). But these large numbers have a disadvantage: Operations on them are slower as greater the number gets. Therefore it is possible to shorten the numbers by using the compiler flag -Dsimplify. For using this option it is required that the underlying number type provides the 'modulo' operation. Information Output during the Computations. If during the algorithm the program should output some information (e.g., during the debugging phase) you can turn on the output information by giving the compiler flag debug. In the file width_assertions.h you can turn on/off the output of some functions and additional information by changing the defined values from 0 (no output) to 1 (output available). But then it is required that the operator<<() has to been overloaded for Point_3 , Plane_3 , Vector_3 and RT . Example Example: Polytope_distance_d/width_simplex.cpp Show / Hide #include <CGAL/Homogeneous.h> #include <CGAL/Width_default_traits_3.h> #include <CGAL/Width_3.h> #include <CGAL/Exact_integer.h> #include <iostream> #include <vector> typedef CGAL::Exact_integer RT; typedef CGAL::Homogeneous<RT> Kernel; typedef Kernel::Point_3 Point_3; typedef Kernel::Plane_3 Plane_3; typedef CGAL::Width_default_traits_3<Kernel> Width_traits; typedef CGAL::Width_3<Width_traits> Width; int main() { // Create a simplex using homogeneous integer coordinates std::vector<Point_3> points; points.push_back( Point_3(2,0,0,1)); points.push_back( Point_3(0,1,0,1)); points.push_back( Point_3(0,0,1,1)); points.push_back( Point_3(0,0,0,1)); // Compute width of simplex Width simplex( points.begin(), points.end()); // Output of squared width, width-planes, and optimal direction RT wnum, wdenom; simplex.get_squared_width( wnum, wdenom); std::cout << "Squared Width: " << wnum << "/" << wdenom << std::endl; std::cout << "Direction: " << simplex.get_build_direction() << std::endl; Plane_3 e1, e2; simplex.get_width_planes (e1, e2); std::cout << "Planes: E1: " << e1 << ". E2: " << e2 <<std::endl; std::cout << "Number of optimal solutions: " << simplex.get_number_of_optimal_solutions() << std::endl; return (0); }

members.name: Point_3
members.brief: point type.

members.name: Plane_3
members.brief: plane type.

members.name: Vector_3
members.brief: vector type.

members.name: RT
members.brief: algebraic ring type.

members.name: ChullTraits
members.brief: traits class for the 3D convex hull algorithm.

members.name: Width_3
members.brief: creates a variable width initialized to the width of $ \mathcal{S}$ - with $ \mathcal{S}$ being the set of points in the range [ first , beyond ).
members.detailed: InputIterator has Point_3 as value type.

members.name: Width_3
members.brief: creates a variable width initialized to the width of the polyhedron $ P$ .
members.detailed: Note that the vertex point coordinates are altered! $ P$ is a convex polyhedron. Polyhedron is a CGAL::Polyhedron_3 with facets supporting plane equations where Polyhedron::Point_3 $ \equiv$ Point_3 and Polyhedron::Plane_3 $ \equiv$ Plane_3 .

members.name: get_squared_width
members.brief: returns the squared width.
members.detailed: For the reason of exact computation not the width itself is stored, but the squared width as a fraction: The numerator in width_num and the denominator in width_denom . The width of the point set $ \mathcal{S}$ is $ \sqrt{\frac{width\_num}{width\_denom}}$ .

members.name: get_width_planes
members.brief: The planes e1 and e2 are the two parallel supporting planes, which distance is minimal (among all such planes).

members.name: get_width_coefficients
members.brief: The returned coefficients A,B,C,D,K have the property that width-plane e1 is given by the equation $ Ax+By+Cz+D=0$ and width-plane e2 by $ Ax+By+Cz+K=0$ .

members.name: get_build_direction
members.brief: returns a direction $ \mathbf{d}_{opt}$ such that the width-planes e1 and e2 are perpendicular to $ \mathbf{d}_{opt}$ .
members.detailed: The width of the point set is minimal in this direction.

members.name: get_all_build_directions
members.brief: All the build directions are stored in the vector dir .
members.detailed: It might happen that a certain body has several different build directions, but it is also possible to have only one build direction.

members.name: get_number_of_optimal_solutions
members.brief: returns the number of optimal solutions, i.e., the number of optimal build directions.

CGAL::Width_default_traits_3.name: CGAL::Width_default_traits_3
CGAL::Width_default_traits_3.brief: The class Width_default_traits_3 is a traits class for Width_3 <Traits> using the three-dimensional CGAL kernel.
CGAL::Width_default_traits_3.detailed: K must be a model for Kernel . Is model of WidthTraits_3 CGAL::Width_3 <Traits> WidthTraits_3

PolytopeDistanceDTraits.name: PolytopeDistanceDTraits
PolytopeDistanceDTraits.brief: This concept defines the requirements for traits classes of $ d$ -dimensional optimization algorithms.
PolytopeDistanceDTraits.detailed: Has models CGAL::Polytope_distance_d_traits_2 <K, ET , NT > CGAL::Polytope_distance_d_traits_3 <K, ET , NT > CGAL::Polytope_distance_d_traits_d <K, ET , NT > CGAL::Polytope_distance_d <Traits>

members.name: Point_d
members.brief: point type used to represent the input points.

members.name: Rep_tag
members.brief: compile time tag to distinguish between Cartesian and homogeneous representation of the input points.
members.detailed: Rep_tag has to be either CGAL ::``Cartesian_tag or CGAL ::``Homogeneous_tag .

members.name: RT
members.brief: number type used to represent the coordinates of the input points.
members.detailed: It has to be a model for RingNumberType .

members.name: FT
members.brief: number type used to return either the squared radius of the smallest enclosing sphere or annulus, or the squared distance of the polytopes.
members.detailed: FT has to be either RT or CGAL ::``Quotient< RT > if the input points have Cartesian or homogeneous representation, respectively (cf. Rep_tag ).

members.name: Access_dimension_d
members.brief: data accessor object used to access the dimension of the input points.

members.name: Access_coordinates_begin_d
members.brief: data accessor object used to access the coordinates of the input points.

members.name: Construct_point_d
members.brief: constructor object used to construct either the center of the smallest enclosing sphere or annulus, or the points realizing the distance between the two polytopes.

members.name: ET
members.brief: exact number type used to do the exact computations in the underlying solver for linear programs.
members.detailed: It has to be a model for RingNumberType . There must be an implicit conversion from RT to ET available.

members.name: NT
members.brief: fast (possibly inexact) number type used to speed up the pricing step in the underlying solver for linear programs.
members.detailed: It has to be a model for RingNumberType . There must be implicit conversions from RT to NT and from NT to ET available.

WidthTraits_3.name: WidthTraits_3
WidthTraits_3.brief: This concept defines the requirements for traits classes of CGAL::Width_3 <Traits> .
WidthTraits_3.detailed: Operations Whatever the coordinates of the points are, it is required for the width-algorithm to have access to the homogeneous representation of points. Has models CGAL::Width_default_traits_3 CGAL::Width_3 <Traits>

members.name: Point_3
members.brief: The point type.
members.detailed: The (in)equality tests must be available. Access to the point coordinates is done via the get_...() functions. Constructing a point is done with the make_point( ) operation.

members.name: Plane_3
members.brief: The plane type.
members.detailed: Access to the coefficients of the plane is made via the get_...() functions. Constructing a plane is done with the make_plane() operation.

members.name: Vector_3
members.brief: The vector type.
members.detailed: There is no need to access the coefficients of a vector; only constructing is required and is done with the make_vector operation.

members.name: ChullTraits
members.brief: The traits class for using the convex hull algorithm.
members.detailed: It must be a model of the concept ConvexHullTraits_3. This class is used only if the width is computed from a set of points.

members.name: RT
members.brief: Ring type numbers.
members.detailed: Internally all numbers are treated as ring type numbers, i.e., neither $ /$ -operator nor $ \sqrt{.}$ nor other inexact operations are used. But because the algorithm does not use any divisions, but multiplication instead, the numbers can get really big. Therefore it is recommended to use a ring type number, that provides values of arbitrary length. Furthermore it is assumed that the underlying number type of Point_3 , Plane_3 and Vector_3 equals RT .

PkgPolytopeDistanceDRef.name: PkgPolytopeDistanceDRef
PkgPolytopeDistanceDRef.detailed: Kaspar Fischer, Bernd Gärtner, Thomas Herrmann, Michael Hoffmann, and Sven Schönherr This package provides algorithms for computing the distance between the convex hulls of two point sets in d-dimensional space, without explicitly constructing the convex hulls. It further provides an algorithm to compute the width of a point set, and the furthest point for each vertex of a convex polygon. Introduced in: CGAL 1.1 BibTeX: cgal:fghhs-od-26a License: GPL Classified Reference Pages All Furthest Neighbors CGAL::all_furthest_neighbors_2() AllFurthestNeighborsTraits_2 Width CGAL::Width_3 <Traits> CGAL::Width_default_traits_3 <K> WidthTraits_3 Polytope Distance CGAL::Polytope_distance_d <Traits> CGAL::Polytope_distance_d_traits_2 <K,ET,NT> CGAL::Polytope_distance_d_traits_3 <K,ET,NT> CGAL::Polytope_distance_d_traits_d <K,ET,NT> PolytopeDistanceDTraits

members.name: all_furthest_neighbors_2
members.brief: computes all furthest neighbors for the vertices of the convex polygon described by the range [ points_begin , points_end ), writes their indices (relative to points_begin ) to o the furthest neighbor of points_begin[i] is points_begin[i-th number written to o] and returns the past-the-end iterator of this sequence.
members.detailed: The function all_furthest_neighbors_2() computes all furthest neighbors for the vertices of a convex polygon $ P$ , i.e. for each vertex $ v$ of $ P$ a vertex $ f_v$ of $ P$ such that the distance between $ v$ and $ f_v$ is maximized. The points denoted by the non-empty range [ points_begin , points_end ) form the boundary of a convex polygon $ P$ (oriented clock- or counterclockwise). The geometric types and operations to be used for the computation are specified by the traits class parameter t . This parameter can be omitted if RandomAccessIterator refers to a point type from a Kernel . In this case, the kernel is used as default traits class. Requires If t is specified explicitly, Traits is a model for AllFurthestNeighborsTraits_2 . Value type of RandomAccessIterator is Traits::Point_2 or, if t is not specified explicitly, K::Point_2 where K is a model for Kernel . OutputIterator accepts int as value type. AllFurthestNeighborsTraits_2 CGAL::monotone_matrix_search() Implementation The implementation uses monotone matrix search [1] . Its runtime complexity is linear in the number of vertices of $ P$ . Example The following code generates a random convex polygon p with ten vertices, computes all furthest neighbors and writes the sequence of their indices (relative to points_begin ) to cout (e.g. a sequence of 4788911224 means the furthest neighbor of points_begin[0] is points_begin[4] , the furthest neighbor of points_begin[1] is points_begin[7] etc.). Example: Polytope_distance_d/all_furthest_neighbors_2.cpp Show / Hide #include <CGAL/Cartesian.h> #include <CGAL/Polygon_2.h> #include <CGAL/point_generators_2.h> #include <CGAL/random_convex_set_2.h> #include <CGAL/all_furthest_neighbors_2.h> #include <CGAL/IO/Ostream_iterator.h> #include <iostream> #include <vector> typedef double FT; typedef CGAL::Cartesian<FT> Kernel; typedef Kernel::Point_2 Point; typedef std::vector<int> Index_cont; typedef CGAL::Polygon_2<Kernel> Polygon_2; typedef CGAL::Random_points_in_square_2<Point> Generator; typedef CGAL::Ostream_iterator<int,std::ostream> Oiterator; int main() { // generate random convex polygon: Polygon_2 p; CGAL::random_convex_set_2(10, std::back_inserter(p), Generator(1)); // compute all furthest neighbors: CGAL::all_furthest_neighbors_2 (p.vertices_begin(), p.vertices_end(), Oiterator(std::cout)); std::cout << std::endl; return 0; }
members.argsstring: (RandomAccessIterator points_begin, RandomAccessIterator points_end, OutputIterator o, Traits t=Default_traits)
